Week 10 - Occupancy Modeling (Instructor)

occupancy
detection probability
imperfect detection
Author

SNR 690

Published

October 1, 2001

1 Week Overview

Week 10 - Occupancy Modeling & Student Presentations (Instructor-Only Plan)

ImportantInstructor-Only Document

This document contains facilitation notes, timing guidance, and pedagogical scaffolding for the instructor. It is not intended for student distribution.

This week introduces single-season occupancy models using a student-led discussion of an MDPI paper on drone-based wildlife surveys. The session emphasizes the fundamental insight that non-detection ≠ absence and how occupancy models account for imperfect detection.

Primary Paper (Student Lecture):

2 Session Structure

NoteNo Session B This Week

This week has Session A only (75 minutes total). There is no Session B. The time normally allocated to Session B has been converted into extended in-class project work time within Session A.

3 Schedule

Time Activity Duration
0:00–0:05 Entry ticket: retrieval practice 5 min
0:05–0:15 Instructor intro + activation (occupancy concepts) 10 min
0:15–0:53 Student Presentations (total ~38 min)
— Paper lecture (Lexie): drone-based monitoring ~31 min
— Progress report (strictly timed) 7 min
0:53–1:00 Challenge questions for presenter + synthesis 7 min
1:00–1:10 Occupancy challenge activity (Think-Pair-Share) 10 min
1:10–1:15 Minute paper + transition to project work 5 min

Total: 75 minutes

4 Session A (75 minutes)

Pre-Class Student Responses

ImportantRequired Before Class

Students must submit written responses to these questions before class (via Canvas or another system). Responses of “I don’t know” or “these methods do not apply” are not acceptable for question 1.

  1. Application to your system: Describe one specific way you could use occupancy modeling or drone-based survey methods in your own research system. Be concrete—name the species/taxon, habitat, and why imperfect detection might matter.

  2. Methods alignment: Does the paper demonstrate logical alignment from research objectives → data collection → statistical methods? Why or why not? (2–3 sentences)

  3. Question for Lexie: Write one substantive question you want to ask the presenter about the paper, methods, or applications.

Instructor facilitation plan for pre-class responses:

  • Before class: Skim all responses; group them by theme (e.g., “detection issues,” “spatial scale,” “cost/logistics,” “statistical concerns”).
  • During entry ticket (0:00–0:05): Project 2–3 anonymized student applications (question 1) as discussion prompts.
  • During Q&A (0:53–1:00): Use student questions (question 3) as the basis for challenge questions. Prioritize questions that probe methodological assumptions.
  • If responses are thin: Have 3 backup challenge questions ready (see below).

Entry Ticket: Retrieval Practice — 0:00–0:05 (5 min)

NoteSoTL Activity: Retrieval Practice

Purpose: Activate prior knowledge from readings and prepare students for occupancy concepts.

Instructions: On your notecard, write answers to:

  1. What is the difference between presence and detection?
  2. If you survey a site once and find nothing, what are two possible explanations?
  3. What is the difference between where an animal is found and where it prefers to be and: what information would you need to tell them apart?

Instructor note: Do not collect these. Have 2–3 students share aloud. Use incorrect/partial answers as teaching moments (error-driven learning).

If students struggle: Prompt with: “Think about a species you’ve tried to find in the field. Was it always there when you looked? Was it always absent when you didn’t see it?”

Instructor Introduction + Activation — 0:05–0:15 (10 min)

Format: Brief instructor-led mini-lecture with slides.

Slide deck: slides/slides-Week10.qmd

Key concepts to cover (slides 1–3):

  1. Occupancy (ψ): Probability a site is occupied by the target species.
  2. Detection probability (p): Probability of detecting the species given it is present.
  3. Detection history: Matrix of 1s (detection) and 0s (non-detection) across sites and visits.
  4. Core insight: A detection history of all 0s could mean:
    • The species is truly absent, OR
    • The species is present but was not detected on any visit.

Activation question (pose to class):

“If you visit a site 5 times and detect nothing each time, what is the probability the species is actually there? What information do you need to answer this?”

Allow 60 seconds for think time. Cold-call 1–2 students. Defer full answer to the challenge activity later.

Student Lecture: Drone-Based Wildlife Monitoring — 0:15–0:46 (~31 min)

Presenter: Lexie

Student Progress Report — 0:46–0:53 (7 min)

Format: Strictly timed 7-minute progress report from a student on their course project.

ImportantStrict Time Enforcement

Use a visible timer. At 6:30, give a 30-second warning. At 7:00, politely but firmly interrupt if necessary. This teaches professional presentation skills and respects class time.

Required content:

  • Current project status
  • Key decisions made since last update
  • One specific challenge or roadblock
  • Next steps

Occupancy Challenge Activity — 1:00–1:10 (10 min)

Format: Think-Pair-Share with small-group discussion.

Slide deck: Use slides 4–6 from slides/slides-Week10.qmd

The Dataset

Present the following detection history matrix. This represents 7 sites surveyed 5 times each for a cryptic species with low detection probability.

Site Visit 1 Visit 2 Visit 3 Visit 4 Visit 5
1 0 1 0 0 0
2 0 0 0 0 0
3 1 0 1 0 0
4 0 0 1 0 0
5 0 0 0 1 0
6 0 0 0 0 0
7 1 1 0 0 1

R code to construct the dataset:

# Detection history matrix for 7 sites, 5 visits
# 1 = detected, 0 = not detected
detection_history <- matrix(
  c(
    0, 1, 0, 0, 0,  # Site 1
    0, 0, 0, 0, 0,  # Site 2
    1, 0, 1, 0, 0,  # Site 3
    0, 0, 0, 0, 0,  # Site 4
    0, 0, 0, 1, 0,  # Site 5
    0, 0, 0, 0, 0,  # Site 6
    1, 1, 0, 0, 1   # Site 7
  ),
  nrow = 7, ncol = 5, byrow = TRUE,
  dimnames = list(
    paste("Site", 1:7),
    paste("Visit", 1:5)
  )
)

detection_history

The Challenge Question

ImportantChallenge

An 8th site is surveyed 5 times. The detection history is: 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 (no detections across all 5 visits).

What is the probability the species is actually present at Site 8?

You may use:

  • The observed data from Sites 1–7
  • Logical reasoning about detection probability
  • Bayes’ theorem (conceptually)

You do not need to compute an exact numerical answer—explain your reasoning.

Think (2 min)

Students work individually. Write down:

  1. Your intuitive estimate of presence probability.
  2. What information from the data did you use?
  3. What assumptions did you make?

Pair (3 min)

Discuss with a partner:

  • Compare your estimates and reasoning.
  • Identify where you agree/disagree.
  • Try to refine your estimate together.

Share (3–4 min)

Instructor calls on 2–3 pairs to share their reasoning. Probe for:

  • How did you estimate detection probability?
  • How did prior occupancy rate inform your estimate?
  • Did you consider that all-zero sites could be true absences OR presence with non-detection?

Instructor Facilitation Notes: Worked Solution Approach

TipHigh-Level Solution Approach (For Instructor Reference)

Key insight: We need to distinguish between P(present | detected) and P(present | not detected).

Step 1: Estimate detection probability (p)

From sites where we know the species is present (Sites 1, 3, 5, 7 — they had at least one detection):

  • Site 1: 1 detection in 5 visits → 1/5 = 0.20
  • Site 3: 2 detections in 5 visits → 2/5 = 0.40
  • Site 5: 1 detection in 5 visits → 1/5 = 0.20
  • Site 7: 3 detections in 5 visits → 3/5 = 0.60

Average detection probability: p ≈ (0.20 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.60) / 4 = 0.35

Or use total: 7 detections / 20 visits at occupied sites = 0.35

Step 2: Calculate probability of all 0s given presence

P(all zeros | present) = (1 - p)^5 = (1 - 0.35)^5 = 0.65^5 ≈ 0.116

This means even if a species IS present, there’s about an 11.6% chance of detecting it zero times in 5 visits.

Step 3: Apply Bayes’ theorem (conceptual)

Let ψ = prior probability of occupancy. From the data, 4 of 7 sites had detections, so naïve estimate is ψ ≈ 4/7 ≈ 0.57. But this underestimates true occupancy because some all-zero sites may be occupied!

Using Bayes:

P(present | all zeros) = [P(all zeros | present) × P(present)] / P(all zeros)

Where: - P(all zeros) = P(all zeros | present)×P(present) + P(all zeros | absent)×P(absent) - P(all zeros | absent) = 1 (if absent, you definitely see zeros)

If we assume ψ = 0.6 (slightly higher than naïve estimate): - P(all zeros) = 0.116 × 0.6 + 1 × 0.4 = 0.0696 + 0.4 = 0.4696 - P(present | all zeros) = (0.116 × 0.6) / 0.4696 ≈ 0.0696 / 0.4696 ≈ 0.15

Answer: Given the observed data, there is approximately a 15% probability that Site 8 is occupied despite having all zero detections.

Key teaching points:

  1. Non-detection ≠ absence. Even with 5 visits, there’s a meaningful chance of missing a present species.
  2. Lower detection probability → higher P(present | all zeros).
  3. Higher prior occupancy → higher P(present | all zeros).
  4. This is why occupancy models are essential for species distribution studies!

If students struggle:

  • Ask: “What fraction of visits resulted in detection at sites where we KNOW the species is present?”
  • Prompt: “If the species were at Site 8, what’s the chance you’d miss it all 5 times?”
  • Simplify: “If detection probability is 0.3, what’s 0.7^5?”

Extensions (if time permits or for advanced students):

  • How does the answer change if p = 0.1 (very cryptic species)?
  • How does the answer change if p = 0.8 (conspicuous species)?
  • What if we added covariates (habitat type affects detection)?

Minute Paper + Transition — 1:10–1:15 (5 min)

NoteSoTL Activity: Minute Paper

Purpose: Metacognitive reflection; identify persistent confusion for next session.

Prompts (on notecard or digital):

  1. What is the single most important insight about occupancy modeling from today?
  2. How might imperfect detection affect a study in your own research area?
  3. One question you still have about occupancy models or the challenge problem.

Instructor action: Collect and skim before next class. Address top 2–3 questions at the start of the next session.

5 In-Class Project Work Time (Integrated Above)

NoteNo Separate Session B

Because there is no Session B this week, structured project work time is integrated into the activities above (the progress report and synthesis discussions).

If the session runs faster than planned (e.g., shorter Q&A), use the remaining time for informal project troubleshooting:

Checkpoint (what students must produce):

  • Identify one specific methodological connection between today’s content (occupancy/detection) and their project.
  • Write 2–3 sentences explaining how they will handle detection/observation error in their analysis (even if the answer is “it’s not relevant because…”).

Peer troubleshooting:

  • Pair students working on similar topics or methods.
  • Each student has 3 minutes to explain their current roadblock.
  • Partner asks one clarifying question and offers one suggestion.

Stretch goal:

  • Students with functioning analyses: Draft a 1-paragraph “methods” description of their detection/observation process.
  • Students still in data collection: Map out their sampling design and identify where imperfect detection might occur.

6 Instructor Reflection Prompts (Post-Class)

After class, note:

  1. Which pre-class responses showed deepest engagement? Patterns?
  2. Did the challenge activity generate genuine Bayesian reasoning, or did students rely on intuition?
  3. How did the presenter handle challenge questions? Areas for feedback?
  4. What persistent confusions emerged from the minute paper?
